reasoning: While the composite fidelity score is high (0.9756), the identified concerns involve data integrity failures in a legal contract. Specifically, the conversion incorrectly substituted 'false' for monetary insurance limits ($2,000,000) and failed to capture 'X' marks in compliance-related form fields. Because these are semantic and data-accuracy errors rather than cosmetic ones, human verification is required per the cost asymmetry policy.

concerns.description: Significant text omission within a section
concerns.analysis: The conversion failed to capture the state of certain critical form fields. In element 62, the question regarding work performed outside the United States is present, but the 'No' answer marked with an 'X' in the source is missing (value is an empty string). This is a significant omission as it relates to offshore performance compliance. Similarly, in element 327, the checkbox for 'Initial Offer' is not captured, although subsequent revised offer selections were captured. These omissions alter t
concerns.source_pdf_quote: Will the Contractor perform any work outside of the United States? __ Yes X No
concerns.location: Participating Addendum, Contractor Utilization of Workers Outside the U.S., Page 5, element index 62

concerns.description: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
concerns.analysis: In the insurance limit tables (elements 1020 and 1026), the output lists the value for 'Each Claim' and 'Annual Aggregate' as 'false'. The source document (page 64) contains monetary limits ($2,000,000). While the values are present in adjacent elements, the inclusion of 'false' in the value field is an incorrect data interpretation that changes the literal meaning of those rows.
concerns.location: Master Agreement, Technology Errors & Omissions Insurance, Page 64, element index 1020

action_items: Verify and manually restore the checkbox 'X' for offshore work compliance (element 62).
Correct the 'Initial Offer' checkbox in element 327.
Fix insurance limit tables (elements 1020, 1026) to reflect the $2,000,000 values found on page 64 of the source.
